The  fall of BJP

# The fall of BJP

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jan 11, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

9

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

The title might seem ironical, considering BJP has been winning elections continously, is in power in around 15 states, as well as the Center, so how exactly it is a fall. The fall is in the fact that it’s no longer the party with a difference, as it once claimed, no longer ideologically different from other parties.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hk7d!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1ce6b973-9d8d-4c14-8931-e85366d64640_310x163.jpeg)

![Narendra Modi and Amit Shah are the big ...](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hk7d!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1ce6b973-9d8d-4c14-8931-e85366d64640_310x163.jpeg "Narendra Modi and Amit Shah are the big ...")

When the BJP rose to prominence in the 1980s–90s, it projected itself as **ideologically distinct** from Congress and other regional parties. It emphasized **discipline, cadre-based organization, cultural nationalism, and clean governance**, contrasting itself against what it called the “dynastic, corrupt” politics of others. This branding gave it moral capital: it wasn’t just another party chasing power, it was a movement with a mission.

In recent years, BJP has relied heavily on **coalition politics, defections, and patronage networks,** the same tactics it once criticized. Like Congress in its heyday, BJP is now seen as revolving around a single towering leader, with diminished inner-party democracy.

Welfare schemes, caste calculations, and regional alliances dominate strategy, mirroring the very “vote-bank politics” it once condemned. The party’s claim of being morally superior has eroded as it faces allegations of cronyism, suppression of dissent, and opportunistic alliances.

Over the last decade, BJP has basically become a personality cult, in sharp contrast to the RSS tradition of collective discipline, ideological training, and cadre loyalty, that discouraged hero worship, emphasizing the organization over individuals.

Modi’s charisma and electoral success in 2014 and 2019 shifted the balance. The BJP became synonymous with Modi, sidelining the RSS’s collective ethos.Many MPs and MLAs rode on Modi’s popularity rather than their own merit. Without his brand, they would struggle even at local levels.

Ministers were often chosen for loyalty rather than competence, leading to a “Yes Boss” culture where independent voices were muted. Except for strong figures like Yogi Adityanath (UP) and Himanta Biswa Sarma (Assam), most state-level leadership was parachuted in by the central command, weakening grassroots legitimacy.

The party lacks a second line of nationally recognized leaders. Every election campaign, slogan, and narrative revolves around him. This creates vulnerability—if Modi’s appeal wanes, the party risks collapse in coherence. BJP once prided itself on being distinct from Congress’s dynastic politics. Ironically, it now mirrors the same centralization and personality-driven model it criticized.

The “Ab ki Baar 400 Paar” slogan itself was audacious: aiming for 400 seats, a number that would eclipse even Rajiv Gandhi’s 1984 landslide. Instead of energizing the cadre, it bred **complacency**. Leaders and workers assumed Modi’s charisma alone would deliver votes, without the need for ground-level mobilization. This was a sharp departure from the BJP’s traditional strength of **RSS-backed grassroots machinery**, which thrives on meticulous booth-level work and voter outreach.

Historically, the Sangh provides detailed ground reports before every election, warning of local discontent, caste dynamics, and regional shifts. The RSS cautioned that complacency and over-centralization were eroding BJP’s connect with voters. To which the party secretary JP Nadda, contemptously declared,“We don’t need the RSS this time, that was not just arrogance, but a symbolic rupture. It signaled that the BJP leadership believed Modi’s image had replaced the need for cadre-based mobilization.

Without RSS’s granular inputs, BJP misread voter sentiment in several states. MPs and MLAs parachuted in by the high command lacked grassroots credibility, and without RSS backing, their campaigns faltered. The party became a one-man show, which magnified both his strengths and his vulnerabilities.

In 2014, 2019, Modi represented change and strong leadership. His face on a poster could swing constituencies, and the RSS’s booth-level machinery ensured that promise translated into votes. The same formula was repeated in 2024, but without the same groundwork. Leaders assumed Modi’s photo was enough, neglecting the **daily visibility and accountability** that voters expect.

Many MPs who won purely on Modi’s name were **absent in their constituencies**, disconnected from local issues. Constituents began to feel neglected, realizing that their representatives lacked independent credibility or initiative. This hollowing out of local leadership meant that Modi’s charisma could no longer mask the **vacuum of governance at the grassroots**.

For decades, the RSS has been the BJP’s backbone, ensuring discipline and ground connect. Watching MPs and leaders grow arrogant and complacent, the RSS felt sidelined. The dismissal of their warnings, combined with the overreliance on Modi’s image, created a **rift**: the Sangh saw the party drifting away from its cadre-based ethos into a hollow personality cult.

Long-time Sangh Parivar workers, who had built credibility at the grassroots, were overlooked. Opportunistic entrants from other parties were parachuted in, often with little ideological alignment or local connect.

The case of Kripashankar Singh in Jaunpur is emblematic. Here was a man who had once released a book titled *“26/11: RSS ki Saazish”*—essentially accusing the Sangh of conspiracy in one of India’s darkest terror attacks. Giving him a BJP ticket was not just tone-deaf, it was a direct insult to the RSS, the very organization that nurtured the party.

RSS cadres, who form the backbone of BJP’s election machinery, were understandably alienated.Voters saw through the opportunism—why reward a defector with no ideological loyalty?

Kripashankar Singh’s defeat by a margin of **5 lakh votes** was not just a loss, it was a resounding rejection of arrogance and poor judgment.

Long-time loyalists and Sangh Parivar workers were brushed aside, replaced by defectors and parachuted names with little ideological grounding. **Disregard for RSS advice**: The Sangh’s ground reports, traditionally the BJP’s compass, were dismissed outright. This wasn’t just tactical arrogance, it was symbolic—declaring that Modi’s aura alone was greater than the organization that birthed the party. The belief hardened that Modi’s image was enough to win, regardless of candidate quality or grassroots connect.

Voters saw through the arrogance. Posters and Modi’s face could not substitute for absent MPs or weak candidates. The cadre, feeling insulted and sidelined, pulled back its full-throttle support. Without booth-level mobilization, BJP’s machinery faltered. The outcome was not just a numerical setback—it was a **symbolic slap**, a reminder that no leader, however charismatic, can replace organizational discipline and grassroots credibility.

Over the last decade, BJP has prided itself on **political engineering**, splitting rivals, pulling down elected governments, and orchestrating defections.These moves were celebrated as “Chanakya Neeti” or “masterstrokes,” projecting the party as tactically invincible.But the RSS, rooted in discipline and ideological purity, has always been uneasy with this opportunism. For them, politics is about **building society**, not just winning power at any cost.

In Maharashtra, BJP already had a comfortable majority with the Shinde-led Shiv Sena. Yet, they brought in Ajit Pawar—an NCP leader tainted by corruption allegations and infamous for opportunistic flip-flops. This wasn’t necessity, it was **gluttony**, A hunger not just to govern, but to dominate, even when dominance was already secured. To the RSS, this was a betrayal of principle, why embrace someone who symbolized the very corruption and opportunism the BJP once claimed to fight.

The BJP’s image as a “party with a difference” collapsed further. RSS workers, who value ideological consistency, saw this as a cynical power grab. Voters began to question whether BJP was any different from Congress or other parties that thrived on defections and opportunism.

Ironically, BJP once railed against the “Lutyens elite” of Delhi, accusing them of sycophancy and disconnect. Yet, over time, it created its own version—a **closed court of flatterers** around the leadership. Genuine ground reports, dissenting voices, and constructive criticism were filtered out. Only praise and “Yes Boss” attitudes reached the top. Leadership became insulated, believing its own propaganda rather than the lived reality of voters and cadres.

Instead of building persuasive campaigns or engaging with issues, the IT Cell devolved into a **trolling machine**.Anyone pointing out flaws—whether opposition, neutral analysts, or even long-time BJP supporters—was abused, hounded, and delegitimized.Unlike RSS cadres who do booth-level mobilization, the IT Cell was digital-only, detached from grassroots realities.

Long-time supporters and ideological loyalists who spoke their minds were sidelined. New entrants, “Johnny-come-lately” right-wing voices post-2014, were given prominence as representatives of the movement. This alienated the very base that had nurtured BJP’s rise, while empowering opportunists who lacked ideological depth or grassroots connect.

It feels like betrayal layered on exhaustion. Imagine the psychology of a lifelong karyakarta. Walking miles to mobilize voters, facing ridicule in hostile states like Bengal and Kerala, sometimes even risking violence.Believing in the idea of a “party with a difference,” rooted in discipline and ideology, not just power. That loyalty and sacrifice would translate into recognition, or at least a voice in decision-making.

The ground reports, the warnings, the lived experiences of cadres are brushed aside by a high command insulated in its “Chota Darbar.” Those who once mocked or abused the RSS suddenly become the “blue-eyed boys,” given tickets, positions, and visibility. The very people who built the foundation feel invisible, sidelined, and even insulted.

Rather than acknowledging mistakes in candidate selection, arrogance, or overreliance on Modi’s image, the leadership appears to be insulating itself further.

Abusing or dismissing the RSS is not just tactical folly—it’s existential. The BJP without RSS’s cadre discipline is like a body without a nervous system. The personality cult around Modi continues to overshadow institutional correction. As long as sycophants dominate the inner circle, feedback loops remain broken.

The BJP’s claim of being “a party with a difference” has eroded; if it continues down this path, it risks becoming indistinguishable from the very parties it once opposed.
